_why: The OVERLAY DIRECTOR (app/overlays.py) reads the storyboard's narration and research and decides WHERE typeset text belongs and WHICH Remotion composition renders it. Wan never draws text (hard rule 2026-07-14); this dictionary is the whole vocabulary of legitimate on-screen text. Edit it to teach the system new text moves — never hardcode one in a pipeline stage.

datestamp.why: A named date or clock time is the documentary's anchor. Stamp it typeset, low-left, on the spoken word.

location.why: Place before event: the viewer must know WHERE before anything happens. Merges with a datestamp in the same scene into one stamp.

person.why: First mention of an integral person gets a name + role tag, so nobody is ever an unlabeled figure on screen.
matches: first mention of a proper name that appears in research facts or the character bible

chapter.why: A signposted time or place jump is a chapter break: full-frame typeset card, cut in like a shot (the study documents this style uses them).

emphasis.why: The one *starred* phrase of a scene, typeset word-by-word on the spoken word. Use sparingly: it is a punch, not a caption track.

callout.why: Annotate a spot in the frame (evidence, a route, a person in a crowd) the way an investigator marks up a photo.

gating._why_share: The reference channel is text-heavy (75-91% of frames), but their text is mostly baked-in subtitles we do NOT copy. Cap our typeset moments so the video reads as designed, not captioned.
